title: Core-Hole Excitation Dynamics of One-Dimensional Ultracold Trapped Fermions
authors: André Becker, Georgios M. Koutentakis, Peter Schmelcher
abstract: We investigate the nonequilibrium dynamics of core-hole excitations in a one-dimensional fermionic few-body system consisting of a spin-polarized Fermi bath coupled to a single heavy mobile impurity. The bath is initially prepared in a particle-hole configuration by emptying a selected bath single-particle orbital, while the impurity is displaced with respect to the center of the bath confinement potential. The quench dynamics are initialized by suddenly switching on the impurity-bath interaction. To resolve the resulting dynamics, we combine two complementary \textit{ab initio} approaches, namely the Multi-Layer Multi-Configuration Time-Dependent Hartree method for mixtures and a multi-channel Born-Oppenheimer framework. We show that the postquench response is governed by the interaction strength, impurity confinement, mass imbalance, and the location of the initially prepared hole within the Fermi sea. The density evolution and impurity center-of-mass motion reveal a competition between mixing and demixing of impurity and bath, while the von Neumann entropy demonstrates the buildup of pronounced many-body correlations. Most importantly, the occupation dynamics of the initially emptied orbital identifies deep core holes as substantially more robust against refilling than bulk or edge vacancies. Our results establish core-hole excitations as robust dynamical many-body features in trapped ultracold fermions and provide a controlled route towards probing orthogonality response, correlation buildup, and hole refilling in real time.
summary: This paper studies how a vacancy (hole) in a 1D Fermi sea behaves when a heavy impurity is suddenly introduced via an interaction quench. The researchers found that holes located at the center of the trap (core holes) are much more stable and resistant to being refilled by other particles than holes at the edges. The study also shows how the impurity's mass and the strength of the interaction drive the buildup of many-body entanglement and the mixing or demixing of the species.
main_problem: Investigating the non-equilibrium, real-time dynamics of core-hole excitations in a one-dimensional fermionic system following an interaction quench.
main_result: Deep core holes are found to be significantly more robust against refilling than bulk or edge vacancies, with the dynamics governed by a competition between impurity-bath mixing and demixing.
method: The study uses two ab initio approaches: the Multi-Layer Multi-Configuration Time-Dependent Hartree (ML-X) method and a multi-channel Born-Oppenheimer (MCBO) framework.
model_or_system: A 1D system consisting of a spin-polarized Fermi bath and a single heavy mobile impurity in harmonic traps, interacting via a delta-function contact potential.
key_observables: One-body densities, impurity center-of-mass motion, von Neumann entropy, and orbital occupation dynamics (hole refilling).
important_parameters: Interaction strength (g), mass imbalance (m_I/m_B), impurity confinement (omega_I), and initial hole position (core, bulk, or edge).
main_assumptions: The interaction is modeled as a delta-function contact potential, and intraspecies interaction in the bath is excluded due to the Pauli principle.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup and initial/dynamic states; Figure 6 displays impurity center-of-mass trajectories; Figure 7 shows von Neumann entropy evolution; Figure 9 demonstrates the convergence of the MCBO method.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the core-hole problem, presents the ML-X and MCBO theoretical frameworks, analyzes the post-quench density and motion dynamics, explores entanglement growth, and concludes with a convergence study of the numerical methods.
why_interesting: It provides a controlled theoretical route to probe orthogonality response, correlation buildup, and real-time hole refilling in many-body systems, which is highly relevant for experiments in quantum gas microscopes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic representation of our setup. (a) Initial non-interacting configuration of the Fermi bath with NB = 5 particles and a hole excitation h, together with a single dis- placed impurity (NI = 1). (b) Dynamics following the inter- action quench g ̸= 0, during which the bath atoms interact with the impurity, resulting in population redistribution and a refilling of the initially empty hole state.

FIG. 2. Spatiotemporal evolution of the coupled bath-impurity dynamics. The top row (ai) displays the bath one-body density ρ(1) B (x; t), the middle row (bi) the associated density difference ∆ρ(1) B (x; t), and the bottom row (ci) the impurity one-body density ρ(1) I (x; t). Parameters are g = 1.0 ℏℓBωB, xs = 2ℓB, mI = 5.0mB, and ωI = 0.5ωB. From left to right, the columns correspond to the initial hole states h = 0 (i = 1, core hole), h = 2 (i = 2, bulk hole), and h = 4 (i = 3, edge hole). The green arrows indicate the density-depleted regions associated with hole initialization.

FIG. 3. Spatiotemporal evolution of the coupled bath-impurity dynamics. The top row (ai) displays the bath one-body density ρ(1) B (x; t), the middle row (bi) the associated density difference ∆ρ(1) B (x; t), and the bottom row (ci) the impurity one-body density ρ(1) I (x; t). Parameters are g = 1.0 ℏℓBωB, xs = 1ℓB, mI = 5.0mB, and ωI = 0.5ωB. From left to right, the columns correspond to the initial hole states h = 0 (i = 1, core hole), h = 2 (i = 2, bulk hole), and h = 4 (i = 3, edge hole).

FIG. 4. Spatiotemporal evolution of the coupled bath-impurity dynamics. The top row (ai) displays the bath one-body density ρ(1) B (x; t), the middle row (bi) the associated density difference ∆ρ(1) B (x; t), and the bottom row (ci) the impurity one-body density ρ(1) I (x; t). Parameters are g = 2.0 ℏℓBωB, xs = 2ℓB, mI = 5.0mB, and ωI = 0.5ωB. From left to right, the columns correspond to the initial hole states h = 0 (i = 1, core hole), h = 2 (i = 2, bulk hole), and h = 4 (i = 3, edge hole).

FIG. 5. Spatiotemporal evolution of the coupled bath-impurity dynamics. The top row (ai) displays the bath one-body density ρ(1) B (x; t), the middle row (bi) the associated density difference ∆ρ(1) B (x; t), and the bottom row (ci) the impurity one-body density ρ(1) I (x; t). Parameters are g = 2.0 ℏℓBωB, xs = 1ℓB, mI = 5.0mB, and ωI = 0.5ωB. From left to right, the columns correspond to the initial hole states h = 0 (i = 1, core hole), h = 2 (i = 2, bulk hole), and h = 4 (i = 3, edge hole).

FIG. 6. Impurity center-of-mass motion ⟨xI(t)⟩for the three hole initial states h = 0, 2, 4. The upper (ai) and lower (bi) rows correspond to g = 1.0ℏωBℓB and g = 2.0ℏωBℓB, respectively. The broken-axis representation enables a comparison of the trajectories for xs = 1ℓB and xs = 2ℓB. The index i = 1 labels the reference setup with mI = 5.0mB and ωI = 0.5ωB, while i = 2 and i = 3 correspond to the heavier-impurity case mI = 10mB and the weaker-trap case ωI = 0.2ωB, respectively.

FIG. 7. Time evolution of the impurity-bath von Neumann entropy SvN(t) for the three hole initial states h = 0, 2, 4. The upper (ai) and lower (bi) rows correspond to g = 1.0ℏωBℓB and g = 2.0ℏωBℓB, respectively. Both values of shift xs = 1ℓB and xs = 2ℓB are provided. The index i = 1 labels the reference setup with mI = 5.0mB and ωI = 0.5ωB, while i = 2 and i = 3 correspond to the heavier-impurity case mI = 10mB and the weaker-trap case ωI = 0.2ωB, respectively.

FIG. 8. Time evolution of the hole-state occupation nh(t) for the three hole initial states h = 0, 2, 4. The upper (ai) and lower (bi) rows correspond to g = 1.0ℏωBℓB and g = 2.0ℏωBℓB, respectively. Both shift values xs = 1ℓB and xs = 2ℓB are provided. The index i = 1 labels the reference setup with mI = 5.0mB and ωI = 0.5ωB, while i = 2 and i = 3 correspond to the heavier-impurity case mI = 10mB and the weaker-trap case ωI = 0.2ωB, respectively.

FIG. 9. Time evolution of the impurity-bath von Neumann entropy SvN(t) for the core-hole initial state with h = 0. The left panel, (a), corresponds to the intermediate interaction strength g = 1.0 ℏωBℓB, while the right panel, (b), shows the stronger interaction case g = 2.0 ℏωBℓB. In each panel, the ML-X result is compared to MCBO calculations with different numbers of PECs.
